title: Terence McKenna's Mega Bad Trip
excerpt: A harrowing mushroom trip in Hawaii between 1988 and 1989 profoundly altered Terence McKenna's relationship with psychedelics, although the truth is more complicated than the long-rumoured story that he stopped tripping after the experience.
content_html:
15 Minute Read

What unfolded during a terrifying mushroom trip in Hawaii between 1988 and 1989 would change Terence McKenna’s relationship with psychedelics – and perhaps reshape the message he shared with the world for the rest of his life.

The full account appears in Graham St. John’s biography, Strange Attractor: The Hallucinatory Life of Terence McKenna, based on extensive interviews and archival work.

Prior to this, the episode lived mostly as rumour and vague anecdotes – touched on in Dennis McKenna’s memoir and a Psychedelic Salon conversation with Bruce Damer – summarised as: McKenna had a trip so bad he basically never tripped again. For a man who popularised the “heroic dose,” this rumour bred charges of hypocrisy.

The reality, as usual, is more complicated.

​

How the Trip Went Down

​

The crisis arrived during what should have been a routine session in the Hawaiian home he shared with his then-wife, Kathleen Harrison. They took their standard five grams of dried psilocybin and lay down on parallel mats. They had followed this ritual monthly since 1982 and weekly for years before, pausing only during Harrison’s pregnancy. On this night, a subtropical storm hit with unusual force. The metal roof, normally a comforting resonator for rain, became deafening.

Harrison reports entering a familiar ecstatic state, a loosening from bodily and domestic burdens. McKenna, by contrast, found himself dragged into what she calls an “outrageous idea space” – “completely uncontrollable, unexpected, ineffable, dense and spacious nothingness.” She could surrender to ego-loss; he could not. In her phrase, he was “really afraid of nothingness.”

As the storm intensified outside, McKenna’s terror escalated inside. Harrison sensed “something dark and scared” to her left; McKenna was curled in the foetal position, vibrating, howling. When language finally returned, it was heavy and minimal: “No… no meaning.” Variations followed: “nothing means anything,” “impossible.” St. John notes that the experience matches classical accounts of a “dark night of the soul”: a collapse of meaning so complete it presents as an abyss of despair, fitting for a man reared in Irish Catholicism.

And for someone who had argued that psychedelics afford genuine contact with the architecture of reality, an encounter with seeming nihilism was devastating.

Harrison tried strategies that had soothed him before – invoking his late mother, a reliable balm during migraines – but hours would pass before he calmed. The next day, he was “livid,” particularly that Harrison had weathered the same conditions and emerged intact while he was reduced to panic. Terence then drew a hard line: he would never trip with Harrison again. He kept that promise. Dennis McKenna later marked this as the start of Terence’s estrangement from his former plant teachers. The “gentle, wise, humorous mushroom spirit” seemed to have turned on him.

​

The Immediate Aftermath

​

After 1988–89, McKenna did not return to mushrooms with his former frequency or bravado. Dennis writes that “he couldn’t deal with it,” that Harrison’s reassurance failed, that “after that experience he very rarely took mushrooms, and he took other psychedelics – such as DMT and ayahuasca – only on rare occasions, and with great reluctance.” The Hawaii trip was not without precedent. Years earlier, McKenna described a specific “place” he would sometimes reach on high-dose psilocybin, which he called “the meatlocker.”

In 1989, speaking at Esalen, he recounted a trip from a few months earlier in which his mental landscape was interrupted by chyrons scrolling across the base of his vision. As he began to read his thoughts displayed before him, he noticed words were misspelt. The text then degenerated into gibberish. He described this as watching his own thoughts “degrade into chaos,” a “visible degradation of meaning.” Elsewhere, he alluded to a Hawaiian trip he simply called “horrible, just horrible.”It also sheds light on why he increasingly preferred to trip alone. Not only did he find others distracting, but his own extreme states could alarm those around him. “I’m sure glad there’s nobody else here to see this,” he reflected, “because… I’m screaming in Urdu, or something.”

In public, though, McKenna continued on occasion to beat the drum for the heroic dose. Harrison and Dennis both identified this as a central contradiction.

In counselling, Harrison pressed him on this gap. He “didn’t want his fans to know that ‘he could be cracked by this thing that he was a master of,’” she said, even while promoting five grams to the inexperienced “without appropriate qualifications on the risks of use.” A further factor was cannabis. Attempts to quit left him feeling stranded in a “horribly profane” world, with a “tremendous narrowing of consciousness.” Substances that once served exploration soon functioned as psychological maintenance. “He just needed to be high on cannabis,” Harrison said. “That was his medicine… what drove everything,” keeping him from “bouncing off the ceiling.”

There were interpersonal and temperamental issues. Harrison observed that McKenna’s cerebral style could bypass tenderness: “I don’t think he ever learned about love and compassion,” she said. “I think he loved his kids and loved me for a while, but I’m not sure he loved himself… he was really afraid of looking at what that means.” Meanwhile, the speaking circuit gathered pace. Through the 1980s, he became a headliner. With success came expectations: constant travel, extended Q&A, and lots more money than he’d enjoyed as an impoverished philosopher and drug dealer in his Berkeley days. Harrison saw his allegiance shifting from family to the public role of psychedelic bard. She would later describe him as a “brilliant narcissist,” whose perfected “rap” had become a prison. The marriage deteriorated and ended bitterly, with heavy emotional and financial costs. As documented in James Kent’s Dose Nation Podcast, Kathleen told Kent that she had become “tired of all the bullshit”.

The timing matters. The late 1980s were a threshold between Cold War certainties and postmodern incredulity. Lyotard’s “incredulity toward metanarratives” became mainstream; inherited meaning-systems (religious, political, philosophical) were widely questioned. Joseph Dewey described an “apocalyptic temper” that intensified as the nuclear standoff receded: a milieu in which McKenna stood as one of culture’s further-out prophets, with his Timewave prophecies increasingly millenarian. Even without the bad trip, the culture around him had shifted.

​

La Chorrera’s Shadow: Prophetic Authority and Pressure

​

McKenna’s crisis likewise cannot be separated from the 1971 “experiment at La Chorrera” in the Colombian Amazon, undertaken with Dennis. Days of high-dose psilocybin with minimal sleep produced for the brothers a vision in which mind and matter interpenetrated; the mushroom intelligence – the Logos – could be contacted and consulted. Back in California, Terence told friends, “history’s going to end any day now.” Friends worried they’d “gone off the rails,” and Dennis later conceded they had a point. In The Invisible Landscape, Terence recalled how friends “preferred to reject us,” as everyone behaved “very oddly” relative to baseline.Terence was explicit that psychedelics generate “funny ideas” – some valid, some interesting, many delusional. “You have to look at them objectively… before you can either dismiss them or accept them”, Dennis has said.

Out of La Chorrera came Timewave Zero: an attempt to formalise the vision using the I Ching, mapping cycles of “novelty” and “habit” across history toward a singularity that would collapse ordinary time (later pegged to 2012). The instinct to use the I Ching had come from what Terence received as direct messages from ‘the Logos’ in later mushroom trips, including arbitrary and specific instructions to segment the oracle’s various patterns in denominations he understood as “elements of time”. In the Logos – a name he drew from Ancient Greek philosophy and the Gospel of St. John – McKenna believed he was in touch with a real personality. The same animist conviction underwrote his love of DMT. After early exposures in the mid-1960s, McKenna declared that understanding the drug would form his life’s work. In The Archaic Revival, he called DMT “so much more powerful, so much more alien” than the rest; “the strongest psychedelic there is.” As Vincent Rado notes, McKenna’s DMT rhetoric is inflected by power: it once empowered his commitment and appalled him. He called it an “intellectual black hole” – the more you understand, the less others can understand you – then added, “but I recommend it.”

He could also be blunt about mania. In a Tripzine interview with James L. Kent, he admitted to “taking roomfuls of people prisoner… for up to 14 hours,” calling it “a sign of mania.” Friends hovered “with a net”, suspicious of the veracity of his Timewave ramblings, which he documented on an enormous and scribble-covered scroll. He once remarked, “I consider myself schizophrenic,” though he preferred to frame his states anthropologically (in shamanic terms) rather than psychiatrically.

​

When Drugs Turn on Their Users

​

Indeed, one of the most revealing threads in McKenna’s worldview – highlighted later by critics like James Kent – is his tendency to treat psychoactive substances not as inert chemicals but as conscious agents. Across psychedelic subcultures, users often speak of drugs as friends, teachers, or tricksters; as LSD, cannabis, or alcohol “turning on” them. Rather than understand their own interiority, the animist tendency may cause drug users to look outside, at the drug itself.

Ashley Lande, reflecting on her own experiences years later, captured this animistic dynamic with brutal honesty: “LSD was my friend. My mercurial, exhilarating, terrifying, abusive friend.” There are accounts of McKenna being later incapacitated by a single sip of mushroom tea at a London party – apparently ingesting the concentrated residue floating on the surface. He confessed: “I don’t want to become afraid of it.” He likened his situation to a mountaineer nearing retirement: “People who climb Mount Everest… at some point they knock off and become a consultant for a sportswear manufacturer or something.”

In contrast to McKenna's seriousness, figures like Hunter S. Thompson and the Grateful Dead approached these drugs with total intellectual irreverence. While its exponents’ lives were far from exemplary, the Pranksterish strain offered resilience to disappointment and confusion. McKenna, by contrast, staked everything on the consistency and profundity of the “heroic dose” and cosmic revelation, which rendered him especially vulnerable to destabilisation when his expectations were betrayed by the unpredictable, sometimes incoherent reality of altered states. A recreational approach might have spared McKenna some of the psychic agony that followed his “bad trip,” allowing for flexible coping rather than existential collapse.

​

Public Rhetoric vs Private Reality

​

The psychological blow of the terror trip came first; the intellectual blow came later.

In the mid-1990s, a young mathematician named Matthew Watkins corresponded with McKenna to discuss flaws in the Timewave Zero calculations, which had metastasised to an ornate pseudo-mathematical computer programme that McKenna believed could finally vectorise his acceptance in the academy as a genius. (While mostly nonsense, McKenna’s colleague Ralph Abraham remarked that Terence had independently discovered certain ideas in chaos theory). They met at the Palenque seminars in 1996. Watkins didn’t arrive as a hostile debunker; he was genuinely curious. Over several friendly conversations, however, he systematically dismantled the mathematics underpinning the theory. Watkins also noticed that the ‘fractals’ now present in McKenna’s graphical constructions were absent in the original formulations from years before, drawing suspicion that the content was changing to fit the raved-up mood of the times.

The timing was difficult. McKenna was suffering from severe migraines, barely able to leave his room. Once back in Hawaii, he posted a formal response on his Hyperborea website, but the encounter clearly shook him. For the first time, someone had challenged his prophetic mathematics on their own terms – and won.

Watkins’s critique came years after the terror trip, so it didn’t cause the initial collapse. But it did deepen McKenna’s scepticism about his own grand narratives. He spent two years in what he called “mathematical hell,” working with physicist John Sheliak to salvage the theory. They could not. By then, the theory’s fate was sealed: it was mathematically baseless.

​

Performance, Doubt, and the Attention Economy

​

Despite his growing doubts, McKenna’s public lectures only became more popular. In private, however, he began to admit what he would not say on stage. To Christian Rätsch and Samten Dorje, he reportedly said he didn’t believe in Timewave Zero anymore: “No. But it pays the bills.” In some regards, his situation resembled contemporary media figures like Alex Jones and Russell Brand, who monetise elaborate ideas and suspicions of the establishment, whether or not they privately believe their own words. As Jules Evans points out, in 1990, Terence charged the equivalent of $7,000 per talk. Like Alan Watts decades earlier, who numbed himself with vodka before dazzling audiences with cosmic aphorisms, McKenna found himself trapped in the role of spiritual entertainer. He knew the risks of audience capture but could not entirely escape them.

Terence himself understood this tension. When Ram Dass once told him, “Your life is your message,” Terence famously replied, “My life is a mess. My message is my message.”

This was not helped by the peculiar power of what one could call McKenna’s “loquacity shield.” Terence was, quite simply, dazzlingly articulate. His long, improvised riffs on history, language, drugs, and consciousness often ran for hours, spinning wild ideas into ornate, seemingly coherent narratives. What might have sounded unhinged in the mouth of someone else – visions of self-transforming machine elves, mushroom Logos, and apocalyptic time fractals – became, through McKenna’s eloquence, compelling philosophical entertainment. This articulate mania had protected him since La Chorrera, where what was arguably a shared psychotic episode between two brothers became the foundation of an elaborate metaphysical system. Later in life, this same verbal brilliance insulated him from scrutiny: few could (or wanted to) puncture the spell. McKenna’s linguistic virtuosity allowed him to present what might otherwise have been dismissed as drug-induced madness as if it were high theory.

​

Into the Memory Hole

​

For all its significance in McKenna’s life story, little was known of it in detail until St. John’s biography was released on October 7th. Dennis originally included some brief details in the first edition of his memoir. Later editions removed the passage entirely, replacing it with a more muted description of Terence’s growing ambivalence and infrequent use of psychedelics.

Why this quiet deletion?

There are perhaps some practical explanations. As Dennis himself remained active on the psychedelic speaking circuit, highlighting that the prophet of the “heroic dose” had been shattered by one might have handed ammunition to prohibitionists or disillusioned McKenna’s followers. But the deeper reason might lie in the cultural dynamics of psychedelic evangelism. As journalist Jules Evans has noted, “psychedelic spokespeople can be prone to audience capture – their audience tends to be younger people who want to hear how healing, meaningful and fun psychedelics are. They don’t want to hear about bad trips.” Some fans were indeed betrayed by the story on first hearing. “For some, it was as if Luke Skywalker suddenly beheld Master Yoda as a malevolent gremlin”, St. John writes.

​

What Makes a Bad Trip?

​

“I am pretty certain he slammed it pre-1990 and earned the right to talk about it in that context,” another commentator wrote on the Psychedelic Salon discussion board. “There is nothing shocking about losing interest in the Mushroom. He just defeated the thing. In the end there was nothing more there to explore from Terence’s point of view.” Although there is no clear evidence that McKenna ever spoke directly about his “terror trip” or integrated lessons from his marriage counselling sessions, he did not shy away from sharing accounts of his difficult experiences in the late 1980s and beyond. Brutal honesty was one of his signature traits, and he often divulged the limits of his own psyche in front of audiences.

He grew increasingly reluctant to serve as a straightforward advocate for mushrooms, noting that they “might not be good for you” and that “there is nothing heroic” about taking them. Yet he continued to valorise the experience of terror itself. “If it doesn’t scare you, it’s not worth doing,” he told audiences. “I will not jump from airplanes, I do not shoot the rapids, I do not rock climb, I’m a bookish person. But I will submit to that terror, because it seems to make sense.” Terror, for McKenna, had its own strange logic. He maintained a heroic attitude toward psychedelics, insisting that “nobody abuses these drugs unless they do too little.”

This dissonance is symptomatic of what Evans calls the “psychedelic religion.” Its central dogma is that psychedelic experiences are inherently meaningful, transformative, and positive. Those who publicly question this – either through critique or through inconvenient personal experiences – risk being ignored, sidelined, or edited out. McKenna’s case is instructive because he wasn’t silenced by external authorities but by the logic of the movement he helped create. The cultural economy surrounding McKenna amplified this dynamic. He was making substantial income from lectures, books, and consultancies – all tied to his role as the witty prophet of the “archaic revival.”

The tragedy and brilliance of Terence McKenna is that this grappling happened in public, under the weight of immense cultural expectation. Most people who experience a shattering bad trip do so in private, integrating the fallout through therapy or spiritual reflection. McKenna had to do it on stage, night after night, while maintaining the persona of someone who had it all figured out.

His loquacity shield could protect him from external challenge, but it could not protect him from the internal collapse triggered by the Hawaii trip.

It’s curious, therefore, that Dennis McKenna has said “there is no such thing as a bad trip”. Recent empirical studies make it impossible to dismiss the existence of truly “bad” psychedelic experiences. In a survey of 613 people who had used classic psychedelics, 8.9% reported functional impairment lasting more than one day, and 2.6% sought medical or psychological assistance following a challenging trip.These numbers may seem small – indeed, the majority report no difficult experiences – but applied across millions of users, they signal a non-negligible risk. Yet even the worst experiences, as merely ‘bad’ as they first appeared, can become ‘challenging’ over time, with negotiation and discernment. Just ask Terence.

They don’t want to hear about bad trips.” Some fans were indeed betrayed by the story on first hearing. “For some, it was as if Luke Skywalker suddenly beheld Master Yoda as a malevolent gremlin”, St. John writes.​What Makes a Bad Trip?​“I am pretty certain he slammed it pre-1990 and earned the right to talk about it in that context,” another commentator wrote on the Psychedelic Salon discussion board. “There is nothing shocking about losing interest in the Mushroom. He just defeated the thing. In the end there was nothing more there to explore from Terence’s point of view.” Although there is no clear evidence that McKenna ever spoke directly about his “terror trip” or integrated lessons from his marriage counselling sessions, he did not shy away from sharing accounts of his difficult experiences in the late 1980s and beyond. Brutal honesty was one of his signature traits, and he often divulged the limits of his own psyche in front of audiences.He grew increasingly reluctant to serve as a straightforward advocate for mushrooms, noting that they “might not be good for you” and that “there is nothing heroic” about taking them. Yet he continued to valorise the experience of terror itself. “If it doesn’t scare you, it’s not worth doing,” he told audiences. “I will not jump from airplanes, I do not shoot the rapids, I do not rock climb, I’m a bookish person. But I will submit to that terror, because it seems to make sense.” Terror, for McKenna, had its own strange logic. He maintained a heroic attitude toward psychedelics, insisting that “nobody abuses these drugs unless they do too little.”This dissonance is symptomatic of what Evans calls the “psychedelic religion.” Its central dogma is that psychedelic experiences are inherently meaningful, transformative, and positive. Those who publicly question this – either through critique or through inconvenient personal experiences – risk being ignored, sidelined, or edited out. McKenna’s case is instructive because he wasn’t silenced by external authorities but by the logic of the movement he helped create. The cultural economy surrounding McKenna amplified this dynamic. He was making substantial income from lectures, books, and consultancies – all tied to his role as the witty prophet of the “archaic revival.”The tragedy and brilliance of Terence McKenna is that this grappling happened in public, under the weight of immense cultural expectation. Most people who experience a shattering bad trip do so in private, integrating the fallout through therapy or spiritual reflection. McKenna had to do it on stage, night after night, while maintaining the persona of someone who had it all figured out. His loquacity shield could protect him from external challenge, but it could not protect him from the internal collapse triggered by the Hawaii trip. It’s curious, therefore, that Dennis McKenna has said “there is no such thing as a bad trip”. Recent empirical studies make it impossible to dismiss the existence of truly “bad” psychedelic experiences. In a survey of 613 people who had used classic psychedelics, 8.9% reported functional impairment lasting more than one day, and 2.6% sought medical or psychological assistance following a challenging trip.These numbers may seem small – indeed, the majority report no difficult experiences – but applied across millions of users, they signal a non-negligible risk. Yet even the worst experiences, as merely ‘bad’ as they first appeared, can become ‘challenging’ over time, with negotiation and discernment. Just ask Terence.

blocks.html_en: What unfolded during a terrifying mushroom trip in Hawaii between 1988 and 1989 would change Terence McKenna’s relationship with psychedelics – and perhaps reshape the message he shared with the world for the rest of his life.

sentences.en: What unfolded during a terrifying mushroom trip in Hawaii between 1988 and 1989 would change Terence McKenna’s relationship with psychedelics – and perhaps reshape the message he shared with the world for the rest of his life.
sentences.zh: 1988 年至 1989 年间在夏威夷进行的一次可怕的蘑菇之旅中发生的事情将改变特伦斯·麦肯纳 (Terence McKenna) 与致幻剂的关系，或许还会重塑他余生与世界分享的信息。

blocks.html_en: The full account appears in Graham St. John’s biography, Strange Attractor: The Hallucinatory Life of Terence McKenna, based on extensive interviews and archival work.

sentences.en: The full account appears in Graham St. John’s biography, Strange Attractor: The Hallucinatory Life of Terence McKenna, based on extensive interviews and archival work.

blocks.html_en: Prior to this, the episode lived mostly as rumour and vague anecdotes – touched on in Dennis McKenna’s memoir and a Psychedelic Salon conversation with Bruce Damer – summarised as: McKenna had a trip so bad he basically never tripped again. For a man who popularised the “heroic dose,” this rumour bred charges of hypocrisy.

sentences.zh: 在此之前，这一事件主要是谣言和模糊的轶事——在丹尼斯·麦肯纳的回忆录和与布鲁斯·达默的迷幻沙龙谈话中提到过——总结为：麦肯纳的旅行非常糟糕，他基本上再也没有绊倒过。

sentences.en: For a man who popularised the “heroic dose,” this rumour bred charges of hypocrisy.
sentences.zh: 对于一个推广“英雄剂量”的人来说，这个谣言引发了虚伪的指控。

blocks.html_en: The reality, as usual, is more complicated.

blocks.html_en: How the Trip Went Down

sentences.en: How the Trip Went Down
sentences.zh: 旅行如何进行

blocks.html_en: The crisis arrived during what should have been a routine session in the Hawaiian home he shared with his then-wife, Kathleen Harrison. They took their standard five grams of dried psilocybin and lay down on parallel mats. They had followed this ritual monthly since 1982 and weekly for years before, pausing only during Harrison’s pregnancy. On this night, a subtropical storm hit with unusual force. The metal roof, normally a comforting resonator for rain, became deafening.

sentences.en: The crisis arrived during what should have been a routine session in the Hawaiian home he shared with his then-wife, Kathleen Harrison.
sentences.zh: 这场危机是在他与当时的妻子凯瑟琳·哈里森在夏威夷的家中进行例行会议时发生的。

sentences.en: They took their standard five grams of dried psilocybin and lay down on parallel mats.
sentences.zh: 他们服用标准的五克干燥裸盖菇素，并躺在平行的垫子上。

sentences.en: They had followed this ritual monthly since 1982 and weekly for years before, pausing only during Harrison’s pregnancy.
sentences.zh: 自 1982 年以来，他们每月都遵循这一仪式，此前几年每周都遵循这一仪式，只在哈里森怀孕期间暂停。

sentences.en: On this night, a subtropical storm hit with unusual force.
sentences.zh: 这天晚上，一场亚热带风暴来袭，威力非比寻常。

sentences.en: The metal roof, normally a comforting resonator for rain, became deafening.
sentences.zh: 金属屋顶通常是雨天的舒适共鸣器，但现在却变得震耳欲聋。

blocks.html_en: Harrison reports entering a familiar ecstatic state, a loosening from bodily and domestic burdens. McKenna, by contrast, found himself dragged into what she calls an “outrageous idea space” – “completely uncontrollable, unexpected, ineffable, dense and spacious nothingness.” She could surrender to ego-loss; he could not. In her phrase, he was “really afraid of nothingness.”

sentences.en: Harrison reports entering a familiar ecstatic state, a loosening from bodily and domestic burdens.
sentences.zh: 哈里森报告说，他进入了一种熟悉的欣喜若狂的状态，身体和家庭负担得到了放松。

sentences.en: McKenna, by contrast, found himself dragged into what she calls an “outrageous idea space” – “completely uncontrollable, unexpected, ineffable, dense and spacious nothingness.”
sentences.zh: 相比之下，麦肯纳发现自己被拖入了她所说的“令人发指的想法空间”——“完全无法控制、出乎意料、难以形容、密集而宽敞的虚无”。

sentences.en: She could surrender to ego-loss; he could not.
sentences.zh: 她可以屈服于自我损失；他不能。

sentences.en: In her phrase, he was “really afraid of nothingness.”
sentences.zh: 用她的话来说，他“真的害怕虚无”。

blocks.html_en: As the storm intensified outside, McKenna’s terror escalated inside. Harrison sensed “something dark and scared” to her left; McKenna was curled in the foetal position, vibrating, howling. When language finally returned, it was heavy and minimal: “No… no meaning.” Variations followed: “nothing means anything,” “impossible.” St. John notes that the experience matches classical accounts of a “dark night of the soul”: a collapse of meaning so complete it presents as an abyss of despair, fitting for a man reared in Irish Catholicism.

sentences.zh: 随着外面的暴风雨愈演愈烈，麦肯纳内心的恐惧也不断升级。

sentences.en: Harrison sensed “something dark and scared” to her left; McKenna was curled in the foetal position, vibrating, howling.
sentences.zh: 哈里森感觉到她左边有“一些黑暗而可怕的东西”；麦肯纳像胎儿一样蜷缩着，颤抖着，嚎叫着。

sentences.en: When language finally returned, it was heavy and minimal: “No…
sentences.zh: 当语言终于恢复时，语气沉重而微弱：“不……

sentences.zh: 没有意义。”

sentences.zh: 随之而来的变化是：“没有任何意义”、“不可能”。

sentences.zh: 圣约翰指出，这种经历与“灵魂的暗夜”的经典描述相吻合：意义彻底崩溃，呈现出绝望的深渊，适合一个在爱尔兰天主教中长大的人。

blocks.html_en: And for someone who had argued that psychedelics afford genuine contact with the architecture of reality, an encounter with seeming nihilism was devastating.

sentences.en: And for someone who had argued that psychedelics afford genuine contact with the architecture of reality, an encounter with seeming nihilism was devastating.
sentences.zh: 对于那些认为迷幻剂可以与现实建筑真正接触的人来说，与看似虚无主义的遭遇是毁灭性的。

blocks.html_en: Harrison tried strategies that had soothed him before – invoking his late mother, a reliable balm during migraines – but hours would pass before he calmed. The next day, he was “livid,” particularly that Harrison had weathered the same conditions and emerged intact while he was reduced to panic. Terence then drew a hard line: he would never trip with Harrison again. He kept that promise. Dennis McKenna later marked this as the start of Terence’s estrangement from his former plant teachers. The “gentle, wise, humorous mushroom spirit” seemed to have turned on him.

sentences.en: Harrison tried strategies that had soothed him before – invoking his late mother, a reliable balm during migraines – but hours would pass before he calmed.
sentences.zh: 哈里森尝试了以前曾安抚过他的策略——向已故的母亲求助，这是治疗偏头痛的可靠药膏——但几个小时后他才平静下来。

sentences.en: The next day, he was “livid,” particularly that Harrison had weathered the same conditions and emerged intact while he was reduced to panic.
sentences.zh: 第二天，他“勃然大怒”，尤其是哈里森经历了同样的情况，而且毫发无伤，而他却陷入了恐慌。

sentences.zh: 特伦斯随后下了一条强硬路线：他再也不会和哈里森一起绊倒了。

blocks.html_en: The Immediate Aftermath

sentences.en: The Immediate Aftermath
sentences.zh: 立即的后果

blocks.html_en: After 1988–89, McKenna did not return to mushrooms with his former frequency or bravado. Dennis writes that “he couldn’t deal with it,” that Harrison’s reassurance failed, that “after that experience he very rarely took mushrooms, and he took other psychedelics – such as DMT and ayahuasca – only on rare occasions, and with great reluctance.” The Hawaii trip was not without precedent. Years earlier, McKenna described a specific “place” he would sometimes reach on high-dose psilocybin, which he called “the meatlocker.”

sentences.en: After 1988–89, McKenna did not return to mushrooms with his former frequency or bravado.
sentences.zh: 1988-89 年之后，麦肯纳并没有像以前那样频繁或虚张声势地回归蘑菇。

sentences.en: Dennis writes that “he couldn’t deal with it,” that Harrison’s reassurance failed, that “after that experience he very rarely took mushrooms, and he took other psychedelics – such as DMT and ayahuasca – only on rare occasions, and with great reluctance.”
sentences.zh: 丹尼斯写道，“他无法应对”，哈里森的保证失败了，“在那次经历之后，他很少服用蘑菇，他只在极少数情况下服用其他迷幻药，例如 DMT 和死藤水，而且非常不情愿。”

sentences.zh: 夏威夷之行并非没有先例。

sentences.en: Years earlier, McKenna described a specific “place” he would sometimes reach on high-dose psilocybin, which he called “the meatlocker.”
sentences.zh: 几年前，麦肯纳描述了他有时会在服用高剂量裸盖菇素时到达的一个特定“地方”，他称之为“肉柜”。

blocks.html_en: In 1989, speaking at Esalen, he recounted a trip from a few months earlier in which his mental landscape was interrupted by chyrons scrolling across the base of his vision. As he began to read his thoughts displayed before him, he noticed words were misspelt. The text then degenerated into gibberish. He described this as watching his own thoughts “degrade into chaos,” a “visible degradation of meaning.” Elsewhere, he alluded to a Hawaiian trip he simply called “horrible, just horrible.”It also sheds light on why he increasingly preferred to trip alone. Not only did he find others distracting, but his own extreme states could alarm those around him. “I’m sure glad there’s nobody else here to see this,” he reflected, “because… I’m screaming in Urdu, or something.”

blocks.html_en: In public, though, McKenna continued on occasion to beat the drum for the heroic dose. Harrison and Dennis both identified this as a central contradiction.

sentences.zh: 不过，在公开场合，麦肯纳有时仍继续鼓吹英雄剂量。

blocks.html_en: In counselling, Harrison pressed him on this gap. He “didn’t want his fans to know that ‘he could be cracked by this thing that he was a master of,’” she said, even while promoting five grams to the inexperienced “without appropriate qualifications on the risks of use.” A further factor was cannabis. Attempts to quit left him feeling stranded in a “horribly profane” world, with a “tremendous narrowing of consciousness.” Substances that once served exploration soon functioned as psychological maintenance. “He just needed to be high on cannabis,” Harrison said. “That was his medicine… what drove everything,” keeping him from “bouncing off the ceiling.”

sentences.zh: 另一个因素是大麻。

sentences.zh: 戒烟的尝试让他感觉自己陷入了一个“可怕的亵渎”世界，“意识极大地狭窄”。

blocks.html_en: There were interpersonal and temperamental issues. Harrison observed that McKenna’s cerebral style could bypass tenderness: “I don’t think he ever learned about love and compassion,” she said. “I think he loved his kids and loved me for a while, but I’m not sure he loved himself… he was really afraid of looking at what that means.” Meanwhile, the speaking circuit gathered pace. Through the 1980s, he became a headliner. With success came expectations: constant travel, extended Q&A, and lots more money than he’d enjoyed as an impoverished philosopher and drug dealer in his Berkeley days. Harrison saw his allegiance shifting from family to the public role of psychedelic bard. She would later describe him as a “brilliant narcissist,” whose perfected “rap” had become a prison. The marriage deteriorated and ended bitterly, with heavy emotional and financial costs. As documented in James Kent’s Dose Nation Podcast, Kathleen told Kent that she had become “tired of all the bullshit”.

blocks.html_en: The timing matters. The late 1980s were a threshold between Cold War certainties and postmodern incredulity. Lyotard’s “incredulity toward metanarratives” became mainstream; inherited meaning-systems (religious, political, philosophical) were widely questioned. Joseph Dewey described an “apocalyptic temper” that intensified as the nuclear standoff receded: a milieu in which McKenna stood as one of culture’s further-out prophets, with his Timewave prophecies increasingly millenarian. Even without the bad trip, the culture around him had shifted.

sentences.zh: 时机很重要。

sentences.en: Joseph Dewey described an “apocalyptic temper” that intensified as the nuclear standoff receded: a milieu in which McKenna stood as one of culture’s further-out prophets, with his Timewave prophecies increasingly millenarian.

blocks.html_en: La Chorrera’s Shadow: Prophetic Authority and Pressure

sentences.en: La Chorrera’s Shadow: Prophetic Authority and Pressure
sentences.zh: 拉乔雷拉的影子：预言的权威和压力

blocks.html_en: McKenna’s crisis likewise cannot be separated from the 1971 “experiment at La Chorrera” in the Colombian Amazon, undertaken with Dennis. Days of high-dose psilocybin with minimal sleep produced for the brothers a vision in which mind and matter interpenetrated; the mushroom intelligence – the Logos – could be contacted and consulted. Back in California, Terence told friends, “history’s going to end any day now.” Friends worried they’d “gone off the rails,” and Dennis later conceded they had a point. In The Invisible Landscape, Terence recalled how friends “preferred to reject us,” as everyone behaved “very oddly” relative to baseline.Terence was explicit that psychedelics generate “funny ideas” – some valid, some interesting, many delusional. “You have to look at them objectively… before you can either dismiss them or accept them”, Dennis has said.

blocks.html_en: Out of La Chorrera came Timewave Zero: an attempt to formalise the vision using the I Ching, mapping cycles of “novelty” and “habit” across history toward a singularity that would collapse ordinary time (later pegged to 2012). The instinct to use the I Ching had come from what Terence received as direct messages from ‘the Logos’ in later mushroom trips, including arbitrary and specific instructions to segment the oracle’s various patterns in denominations he understood as “elements of time”. In the Logos – a name he drew from Ancient Greek philosophy and the Gospel of St. John – McKenna believed he was in touch with a real personality. The same animist conviction underwrote his love of DMT. After early exposures in the mid-1960s, McKenna declared that understanding the drug would form his life’s work. In The Archaic Revival, he called DMT “so much more powerful, so much more alien” than the rest; “the strongest psychedelic there is.” As Vincent Rado notes, McKenna’s DMT rhetoric is inflected by power: it once empowered his commitment and appalled him. He called it an “intellectual black hole” – the more you understand, the less others can understand you – then added, “but I recommend it.”

blocks.html_en: Indeed, one of the most revealing threads in McKenna’s worldview – highlighted later by critics like James Kent – is his tendency to treat psychoactive substances not as inert chemicals but as conscious agents. Across psychedelic subcultures, users often speak of drugs as friends, teachers, or tricksters; as LSD, cannabis, or alcohol “turning on” them. Rather than understand their own interiority, the animist tendency may cause drug users to look outside, at the drug itself.

He confessed: “I don’t want to become afraid of it.” He likened his situation to a mountaineer nearing retirement: “People who climb Mount Everest… at some point they knock off and become a consultant for a sportswear manufacturer or something.”

blocks.html_en: In contrast to McKenna's seriousness, figures like Hunter S. Thompson and the Grateful Dead approached these drugs with total intellectual irreverence. While its exponents’ lives were far from exemplary, the Pranksterish strain offered resilience to disappointment and confusion. McKenna, by contrast, staked everything on the consistency and profundity of the “heroic dose” and cosmic revelation, which rendered him especially vulnerable to destabilisation when his expectations were betrayed by the unpredictable, sometimes incoherent reality of altered states. A recreational approach might have spared McKenna some of the psychic agony that followed his “bad trip,” allowing for flexible coping rather than existential collapse.

blocks.html_en: The psychological blow of the terror trip came first; the intellectual blow came later.

blocks.html_en: In the mid-1990s, a young mathematician named Matthew Watkins corresponded with McKenna to discuss flaws in the Timewave Zero calculations, which had metastasised to an ornate pseudo-mathematical computer programme that McKenna believed could finally vectorise his acceptance in the academy as a genius. (While mostly nonsense, McKenna’s colleague Ralph Abraham remarked that Terence had independently discovered certain ideas in chaos theory). They met at the Palenque seminars in 1996. Watkins didn’t arrive as a hostile debunker; he was genuinely curious. Over several friendly conversations, however, he systematically dismantled the mathematics underpinning the theory. Watkins also noticed that the ‘fractals’ now present in McKenna’s graphical constructions were absent in the original formulations from years before, drawing suspicion that the content was changing to fit the raved-up mood of the times.

blocks.html_en: Despite his growing doubts, McKenna’s public lectures only became more popular. In private, however, he began to admit what he would not say on stage. To Christian Rätsch and Samten Dorje, he reportedly said he didn’t believe in Timewave Zero anymore: “No. But it pays the bills.” In some regards, his situation resembled contemporary media figures like Alex Jones and Russell Brand, who monetise elaborate ideas and suspicions of the establishment, whether or not they privately believe their own words. As Jules Evans points out, in 1990, Terence charged the equivalent of $7,000 per talk. Like Alan Watts decades earlier, who numbed himself with vodka before dazzling audiences with cosmic aphorisms, McKenna found himself trapped in the role of spiritual entertainer. He knew the risks of audience capture but could not entirely escape them.

blocks.html_en: This was not helped by the peculiar power of what one could call McKenna’s “loquacity shield.” Terence was, quite simply, dazzlingly articulate. His long, improvised riffs on history, language, drugs, and consciousness often ran for hours, spinning wild ideas into ornate, seemingly coherent narratives. What might have sounded unhinged in the mouth of someone else – visions of self-transforming machine elves, mushroom Logos, and apocalyptic time fractals – became, through McKenna’s eloquence, compelling philosophical entertainment. This articulate mania had protected him since La Chorrera, where what was arguably a shared psychotic episode between two brothers became the foundation of an elaborate metaphysical system. Later in life, this same verbal brilliance insulated him from scrutiny: few could (or wanted to) puncture the spell. McKenna’s linguistic virtuosity allowed him to present what might otherwise have been dismissed as drug-induced madness as if it were high theory.

sentences.zh: 在别人嘴里听起来可能有些疯狂的东西——自我改造的机器精灵、蘑菇标志和世界末日的时间分形——通过麦肯纳的雄辩，变成了引人入胜的哲学娱乐。

sentences.en: This articulate mania had protected him since La Chorrera, where what was arguably a shared psychotic episode between two brothers became the foundation of an elaborate metaphysical system.
sentences.zh: 自《拉乔雷拉》以来，这种清晰的狂热一直保护着他，在《拉乔雷拉》中，可以说是两兄弟之间共同的精神病发作成为了一个复杂的形而上学体系的基础。

sentences.en: Later in life, this same verbal brilliance insulated him from scrutiny: few could (or wanted to) puncture the spell.
sentences.zh: 在后来的生活中，同样的言语才华使他免受审查：很少有人能够（或想要）刺破这个咒语。

sentences.en: McKenna’s linguistic virtuosity allowed him to present what might otherwise have been dismissed as drug-induced madness as if it were high theory.
sentences.zh: 麦肯纳精湛的语言技巧使他能够将原本可能被视为药物引起的疯狂的现象呈现为高深的理论。

blocks.html_en: Into the Memory Hole

sentences.en: Into the Memory Hole
sentences.zh: 进入记忆洞

blocks.html_en: For all its significance in McKenna’s life story, little was known of it in detail until St. John’s biography was released on October 7th. Dennis originally included some brief details in the first edition of his memoir. Later editions removed the passage entirely, replacing it with a more muted description of Terence’s growing ambivalence and infrequent use of psychedelics.

sentences.en: For all its significance in McKenna’s life story, little was known of it in detail until St. John’s biography was released on October 7th.
sentences.zh: 尽管它在麦肯纳的人生故事中具有重要意义，但直到 10 月 7 日圣约翰传记发布之前，人们对其细节知之甚少。

sentences.en: Dennis originally included some brief details in the first edition of his memoir.
sentences.zh: 丹尼斯最初在他的回忆录第一版中包含了一些简短的细节。

sentences.en: Later editions removed the passage entirely, replacing it with a more muted description of Terence’s growing ambivalence and infrequent use of psychedelics.
sentences.zh: 后来的版本完全删除了这段话，取而代之的是对特伦斯日益增长的矛盾心理和不经常使用迷幻药的更温和的描述。

blocks.html_en: Why this quiet deletion?

sentences.en: Why this quiet deletion?
sentences.zh: 为什么要悄悄删除？

blocks.html_en: There are perhaps some practical explanations. As Dennis himself remained active on the psychedelic speaking circuit, highlighting that the prophet of the “heroic dose” had been shattered by one might have handed ammunition to prohibitionists or disillusioned McKenna’s followers. But the deeper reason might lie in the cultural dynamics of psychedelic evangelism. As journalist Jules Evans has noted, “psychedelic spokespeople can be prone to audience capture – their audience tends to be younger people who want to hear how healing, meaningful and fun psychedelics are. They don’t want to hear about bad trips.” Some fans were indeed betrayed by the story on first hearing. “For some, it was as if Luke Skywalker suddenly beheld Master Yoda as a malevolent gremlin”, St. John writes.

sentences.en: There are perhaps some practical explanations.
sentences.zh: 也许有一些实际的解释。

sentences.en: As Dennis himself remained active on the psychedelic speaking circuit, highlighting that the prophet of the “heroic dose” had been shattered by one might have handed ammunition to prohibitionists or disillusioned McKenna’s followers.
sentences.zh: 由于丹尼斯本人仍然活跃在迷幻演讲中，他强调“英雄剂量”的预言家已经被一个可能向禁酒主义者提供弹药或让麦肯纳的追随者幻灭的人粉碎了。

sentences.en: But the deeper reason might lie in the cultural dynamics of psychedelic evangelism.
sentences.zh: 但更深层次的原因可能在于迷幻布道的文化动力。

sentences.en: As journalist Jules Evans has noted, “psychedelic spokespeople can be prone to audience capture – their audience tends to be younger people who want to hear how healing, meaningful and fun psychedelics are.
sentences.zh: 正如记者朱尔斯·埃文斯 (Jules Evans) 所指出的，“迷幻剂代言人很容易吸引观众——他们的观众往往是年轻人，他们想听听迷幻剂是如何治愈、有意义和有趣的。

sentences.en: They don’t want to hear about bad trips.”
sentences.zh: 他们不想听到糟糕的旅行。”

sentences.en: Some fans were indeed betrayed by the story on first hearing.
sentences.zh: 有些粉丝在第一次听到这个故事时确实被背叛了。

sentences.zh: “对于某些人来说，卢克·天行者突然将尤达大师视为恶毒的小魔怪”，圣约翰写道。

blocks.html_en: What Makes a Bad Trip?

sentences.en: What Makes a Bad Trip?
sentences.zh: 是什么导致了一次糟糕的旅行？

blocks.html_en: “I am pretty certain he slammed it pre-1990 and earned the right to talk about it in that context,” another commentator wrote on the Psychedelic Salon discussion board. “There is nothing shocking about losing interest in the Mushroom. He just defeated the thing. In the end there was nothing more there to explore from Terence’s point of view.” Although there is no clear evidence that McKenna ever spoke directly about his “terror trip” or integrated lessons from his marriage counselling sessions, he did not shy away from sharing accounts of his difficult experiences in the late 1980s and beyond. Brutal honesty was one of his signature traits, and he often divulged the limits of his own psyche in front of audiences.

sentences.en: “I am pretty certain he slammed it pre-1990 and earned the right to talk about it in that context,” another commentator wrote on the Psychedelic Salon discussion board.
sentences.zh: 另一位评论员在迷幻沙龙讨论板上写道：“我非常确定他在 1990 年之前就抨击了它，并赢得了在这种背景下谈论它的权利。”

sentences.en: “There is nothing shocking about losing interest in the Mushroom.
sentences.zh: “对蘑菇失去兴趣并没有什么令人震惊的。

sentences.en: He just defeated the thing.
sentences.zh: 他刚刚击败了那件事。

sentences.en: In the end there was nothing more there to explore from Terence’s point of view.”
sentences.zh: 最终，从特伦斯的角度来看，没有什么可以探索的了。”

sentences.en: Although there is no clear evidence that McKenna ever spoke directly about his “terror trip” or integrated lessons from his marriage counselling sessions, he did not shy away from sharing accounts of his difficult experiences in the late 1980s and beyond.
sentences.zh: 尽管没有明确的证据表明麦肯纳曾直接谈论过他的“恐怖之旅”或综合了他从婚姻咨询课程中获得的教训，但他并没有回避分享他在 20 世纪 80 年代末及以后的艰难经历。

sentences.en: Brutal honesty was one of his signature traits, and he often divulged the limits of his own psyche in front of audiences.
sentences.zh: 残酷的诚实是他的标志性特征之一，他经常在观众面前透露自己心理的局限性。

blocks.html_en: He grew increasingly reluctant to serve as a straightforward advocate for mushrooms, noting that they “might not be good for you” and that “there is nothing heroic” about taking them. Yet he continued to valorise the experience of terror itself. “If it doesn’t scare you, it’s not worth doing,” he told audiences. “I will not jump from airplanes, I do not shoot the rapids, I do not rock climb, I’m a bookish person. But I will submit to that terror, because it seems to make sense.” Terror, for McKenna, had its own strange logic. He maintained a heroic attitude toward psychedelics, insisting that “nobody abuses these drugs unless they do too little.”

sentences.en: He grew increasingly reluctant to serve as a straightforward advocate for mushrooms, noting that they “might not be good for you” and that “there is nothing heroic” about taking them.
sentences.zh: 他越来越不愿意直接充当蘑菇的倡导者，并指出蘑菇“可能对你没有好处”，并且服用蘑菇“没有什么英雄主义”。

sentences.en: Yet he continued to valorise the experience of terror itself.
sentences.zh: 然而他继续重视恐怖体验本身。

sentences.en: “If it doesn’t scare you, it’s not worth doing,” he told audiences.
sentences.zh: “如果它不吓到你，那就不值得做，”他告诉观众。

sentences.en: “I will not jump from airplanes, I do not shoot the rapids, I do not rock climb, I’m a bookish person.
sentences.zh: “我不会从飞机上跳下来，我不会拍摄急流，我不会攀岩，我是一个书生气十足的人。

sentences.en: But I will submit to that terror, because it seems to make sense.”
sentences.zh: 但我会屈服于这种恐惧，因为它似乎是有道理的。”

sentences.en: Terror, for McKenna, had its own strange logic.
sentences.zh: 对于麦肯纳来说，恐怖有其独特的逻辑。

sentences.en: He maintained a heroic attitude toward psychedelics, insisting that “nobody abuses these drugs unless they do too little.”
sentences.zh: 他对迷幻药保持着英雄般的态度，坚持认为“没有人滥用这些药物，除非它们做得太少。”

blocks.html_en: This dissonance is symptomatic of what Evans calls the “psychedelic religion.” Its central dogma is that psychedelic experiences are inherently meaningful, transformative, and positive. Those who publicly question this – either through critique or through inconvenient personal experiences – risk being ignored, sidelined, or edited out. McKenna’s case is instructive because he wasn’t silenced by external authorities but by the logic of the movement he helped create. The cultural economy surrounding McKenna amplified this dynamic. He was making substantial income from lectures, books, and consultancies – all tied to his role as the witty prophet of the “archaic revival.”

sentences.en: This dissonance is symptomatic of what Evans calls the “psychedelic religion.”
sentences.zh: 这种不和谐是埃文斯所说的“迷幻宗教”的症状。

sentences.en: Its central dogma is that psychedelic experiences are inherently meaningful, transformative, and positive.
sentences.zh: 它的中心教条是迷幻体验本质上是有意义的、变革性的和积极的。

sentences.en: Those who publicly question this – either through critique or through inconvenient personal experiences – risk being ignored, sidelined, or edited out.
sentences.zh: 那些公开质疑这一点的人——无论是通过批评还是通过不方便的个人经历——都有被忽视、边缘化或被删除的风险。

sentences.en: McKenna’s case is instructive because he wasn’t silenced by external authorities but by the logic of the movement he helped create.
sentences.zh: 麦肯纳的案例很有启发性，因为他不是被外部权威压制，而是被他帮助发起的运动的逻辑压制。

sentences.en: The cultural economy surrounding McKenna amplified this dynamic.
sentences.zh: 麦肯纳周围的文化经济放大了这种动力。

sentences.en: He was making substantial income from lectures, books, and consultancies – all tied to his role as the witty prophet of the “archaic revival.”
sentences.zh: 他通过讲座、书籍和咨询获得了可观的收入——所有这些都与他作为“古老复兴”的机智先知的角色息息相关。

blocks.html_en: The tragedy and brilliance of Terence McKenna is that this grappling happened in public, under the weight of immense cultural expectation. Most people who experience a shattering bad trip do so in private, integrating the fallout through therapy or spiritual reflection. McKenna had to do it on stage, night after night, while maintaining the persona of someone who had it all figured out.

sentences.en: The tragedy and brilliance of Terence McKenna is that this grappling happened in public, under the weight of immense cultural expectation.
sentences.zh: 特伦斯·麦肯纳的悲剧和辉煌之处在于，这场斗争是在巨大的文化期望的重压下在公共场合发生的。

sentences.en: Most people who experience a shattering bad trip do so in private, integrating the fallout through therapy or spiritual reflection.
sentences.zh: 大多数经历过一次令人震惊的糟糕旅行的人都是私下里经历的，通过治疗或精神反思来整合后果。

sentences.en: McKenna had to do it on stage, night after night, while maintaining the persona of someone who had it all figured out.
sentences.zh: 麦肯纳必须一夜又一夜地在舞台上表演，同时保持一个已经解决了一切的人的形象。

blocks.html_en: His loquacity shield could protect him from external challenge, but it could not protect him from the internal collapse triggered by the Hawaii trip.

sentences.en: His loquacity shield could protect him from external challenge, but it could not protect him from the internal collapse triggered by the Hawaii trip.
sentences.zh: 他的多嘴盾牌可以保护他免受外部挑战，但却无法保护他免受夏威夷之行引发的内心崩溃。

blocks.html_en: It’s curious, therefore, that Dennis McKenna has said “there is no such thing as a bad trip”. Recent empirical studies make it impossible to dismiss the existence of truly “bad” psychedelic experiences. In a survey of 613 people who had used classic psychedelics, 8.9% reported functional impairment lasting more than one day, and 2.6% sought medical or psychological assistance following a challenging trip.These numbers may seem small – indeed, the majority report no difficult experiences – but applied across millions of users, they signal a non-negligible risk. Yet even the worst experiences, as merely ‘bad’ as they first appeared, can become ‘challenging’ over time, with negotiation and discernment. Just ask Terence.

sentences.en: It’s curious, therefore, that Dennis McKenna has said “there is no such thing as a bad trip”.
sentences.zh: 因此，奇怪的是，丹尼斯·麦肯纳说过“没有糟糕的旅行”。

sentences.en: Recent empirical studies make it impossible to dismiss the existence of truly “bad” psychedelic experiences.
sentences.zh: 最近的实证研究表明我们不可能否认真正“糟糕”的迷幻体验的存在。

sentences.en: In a survey of 613 people who had used classic psychedelics, 8.9% reported functional impairment lasting more than one day, and 2.6% sought medical or psychological assistance following a challenging trip.These numbers may seem small – indeed, the majority report no difficult experiences – but applied across millions of users, they signal a non-negligible risk.
sentences.zh: 在一项针对 613 名使用过经典致幻剂的人的调查中，8.9% 的人报告功能障碍持续超过一天，2.6% 的人在一次充满挑战的旅行后寻求医疗或心理援助。这些数字可能看起来很小——事实上，大多数人报告没有困难的经历——但应用于数百万用户，它们表明存在不可忽视的风险。

sentences.en: Yet even the worst experiences, as merely ‘bad’ as they first appeared, can become ‘challenging’ over time, with negotiation and discernment.
sentences.zh: 然而，即使是最糟糕的经历，一开始看起来只是“糟糕”，但随着时间的推移，通过谈判和洞察力，也可能变得“具有挑战性”。

sentences.en: Just ask Terence.
sentences.zh: 问问特伦斯就知道了。

content_html_zh:
15 分钟阅读

1988 年至 1989 年间在夏威夷进行的一次可怕的蘑菇之旅中发生的事情将改变特伦斯·麦肯纳 (Terence McKenna) 与致幻剂的关系，或许还会重塑他余生与世界分享的信息。

完整的叙述出现在格雷厄姆·圣约翰的传记《奇怪的吸引子：特伦斯·麦肯纳的幻觉生活》中，该书基于广泛的采访和档案工作。

在此之前，这一事件主要是谣言和模糊的轶事——在丹尼斯·麦肯纳的回忆录和与布鲁斯·达默的迷幻沙龙谈话中提到过——总结为：麦肯纳的旅行非常糟糕，他基本上再也没有绊倒过。 对于一个推广“英雄剂量”的人来说，这个谣言引发了虚伪的指控。

与往常一样，现实要复杂得多。

​

旅行如何进行

​

这场危机是在他与当时的妻子凯瑟琳·哈里森在夏威夷的家中进行例行会议时发生的。 他们服用标准的五克干燥裸盖菇素，并躺在平行的垫子上。 自 1982 年以来，他们每月都遵循这一仪式，此前几年每周都遵循这一仪式，只在哈里森怀孕期间暂停。 这天晚上，一场亚热带风暴来袭，威力非比寻常。 金属屋顶通常是雨天的舒适共鸣器，但现在却变得震耳欲聋。

哈里森报告说，他进入了一种熟悉的欣喜若狂的状态，身体和家庭负担得到了放松。 相比之下，麦肯纳发现自己被拖入了她所说的“令人发指的想法空间”——“完全无法控制、出乎意料、难以形容、密集而宽敞的虚无”。 她可以屈服于自我损失；他不能。 用她的话来说，他“真的害怕虚无”。

随着外面的暴风雨愈演愈烈，麦肯纳内心的恐惧也不断升级。 哈里森感觉到她左边有“一些黑暗而可怕的东西”；麦肯纳像胎儿一样蜷缩着，颤抖着，嚎叫着。 当语言终于恢复时，语气沉重而微弱：“不…… 没有意义。” 随之而来的变化是：“没有任何意义”、“不可能”。 圣约翰指出，这种经历与“灵魂的暗夜”的经典描述相吻合：意义彻底崩溃，呈现出绝望的深渊，适合一个在爱尔兰天主教中长大的人。

对于那些认为迷幻剂可以与现实建筑真正接触的人来说，与看似虚无主义的遭遇是毁灭性的。

哈里森尝试了以前曾安抚过他的策略——向已故的母亲求助，这是治疗偏头痛的可靠药膏——但几个小时后他才平静下来。 第二天，他“勃然大怒”，尤其是哈里森经历了同样的情况，而且毫发无伤，而他却陷入了恐慌。 特伦斯随后下了一条强硬路线：他再也不会和哈里森一起绊倒了。 他遵守了这个诺言。 丹尼斯·麦肯纳后来将此标记为特伦斯与他以前的植物老师疏远的开始。 “温柔、睿智、幽默的蘑菇精灵”似乎对他产生了兴趣。

​

立即的后果

​

1988-89 年之后，麦肯纳并没有像以前那样频繁或虚张声势地回归蘑菇。 丹尼斯写道，“他无法应对”，哈里森的保证失败了，“在那次经历之后，他很少服用蘑菇，他只在极少数情况下服用其他迷幻药，例如 DMT 和死藤水，而且非常不情愿。” 夏威夷之行并非没有先例。 几年前，麦肯纳描述了他有时会在服用高剂量裸盖菇素时到达的一个特定“地方”，他称之为“肉柜”。

1989 年，在伊莎伦演讲时，他讲述了几个月前的一次旅行，当时他的精神景观被在他视野底部滚动的希伦子打断了。 当他开始阅读他面前的想法时，他注意到单词拼写错误。 然后文本就变成了胡言乱语。 他将此描述为看着自己的思想“陷入混乱”，“意义的明显退化”。 在其他地方，他提到了一次夏威夷旅行，他简单地称之为“可怕，简直可怕”。这也解释了为什么他越来越喜欢独自旅行。 他不仅发现其他人分散了他的注意力，而且他自己的极端状态也会让周围的人感到震惊。 “我很高兴这里没有其他人看到这个，”他反思道，“因为…… 我用乌尔都语或者别的什么尖叫。”

不过，在公开场合，麦肯纳有时仍继续鼓吹英雄剂量。 哈里森和丹尼斯都认为这是一个核心矛盾。

在咨询中，哈里森向他提出了这个问题。 她说，他“不想让他的粉丝知道，‘他可能会被他擅长的这个东西搞垮’”，尽管他向“没有适当的使用风险资格”的缺乏经验的人推销 5 克。 另一个因素是大麻。 戒烟的尝试让他感觉自己陷入了一个“可怕的亵渎”世界，“意识极大地狭窄”。 曾经用于探索的物质很快就起到了心理维持的作用。 “他只需要吸食大麻就可以了，”哈里森说。 “那是他的药…… 是什么驱动了一切，”让他免于“从天花板上弹下来”。

存在人际关系和脾气问题。 哈里森观察到麦肯纳的理性风格可以绕过温柔：“我认为他从未了解过爱和同情心，”她说。 “我认为他爱他的孩子，也爱过我一段时间，但我不确定他是否爱自己…… 他真的很害怕看到这意味着什么。” 与此同时，巡回演讲的步伐加快了。 整个 20 世纪 80 年代，他成为了头条新闻。 成功带来了期望：不断的旅行、延长的问答时间，以及比他在伯克利时代作为一个贫穷的哲学家和毒贩所享受的更多的钱。 哈里森发现自己的忠诚度从家庭转向了迷幻吟游诗人的公共角色。 她后来形容他是一个“才华横溢的自恋者”，他完美的“说唱”已经成为一座监狱。 这段婚姻恶化并以痛苦结束，带来了沉重的情感和经济代价。 正如詹姆斯·肯特的 Dose Nation 播客中记录的那样，凯瑟琳告诉肯特，她已经“厌倦了所有的废话”。

时机很重要。 20 世纪 80 年代末是冷战确定性与后现代怀疑性之间的一个门槛。 利奥塔的“对元叙事的怀疑”成为主流；继承的意义体系（宗教、政治、哲学）受到广泛质疑。 约瑟夫·杜威描述了一种“世界末日般的脾气”，这种脾气随着核对峙的消退而加剧：在这种环境中，麦肯纳是文化中最先进的预言家之一，他的时间波预言越来越千禧年化。 即使没有那次糟糕的旅行，他周围的文化也已经发生了变化。

​

拉乔雷拉的影子：预言的权威和压力

​

麦肯纳的危机同样与 1971 年与丹尼斯在哥伦比亚亚马逊进行的“拉乔雷拉实验”密不可分。 连续几天服用高剂量的裸盖菇素和极少的睡眠，给兄弟俩带来了心灵和物质相互渗透的幻象。蘑菇智慧——Logos——可以被联系和咨询。 回到加利福尼亚后，特伦斯告诉朋友们，“历史随时都会结束。” 朋友们担心他们“偏离了轨道”，丹尼斯后来承认他们有道理。 在《看不见的风景》中，特伦斯回忆起朋友们如何“更愿意拒绝我们”，因为每个人的行为相对于基线都“非常奇怪”。特伦斯明确表示迷幻药会产生“有趣的想法”——有些是有效的，有些是有趣的，很多是妄想。 “你必须客观地看待他们...... 在你可以解雇他们或接受他们之前”，丹尼斯说。

时间波零（Timewave Zero）源自拉乔雷拉（La Chorrera）：试图利用《易经》将这一愿景形式化，将历史上“新奇”和“习惯”的循环映射到一个会崩溃平常时间的奇点（后来与 2012 年挂钩）。 使用《易经》的本能来自于特伦斯在后来的蘑菇之旅中从“理则”那里收到的直接信息，其中包括任意和具体的指令，以将神谕的各种模式划分为他理解为“时间元素”的面额。 麦肯纳相信，在“逻各斯”（Logos）这个名字中，他从古希腊哲学和《圣约翰福音》中汲取了一个名字，他接触到了一个真实的人格。 同样的万物有灵论信念也支撑着他对 DMT 的热爱。 在 20 世纪 60 年代中期早期接触该药物后，麦肯纳宣称了解这种药物将成为他一生的工作。 在《古风复兴》一书中，他称 DMT 比其他理论“更强大、更陌生”。 “最强的迷幻剂。” 正如文森特·拉多 (Vincent Rado) 指出的那样，麦肯纳的 DMT 言论受到了权力的影响：权力曾经赋予他承诺的力量，但也让他感到震惊。 他称其为“智力黑洞”——你理解得越多，别人就越难理解你——然后补充道，“但我推荐它。”

他也可以直言不讳地谈论躁狂症。 在 Tripzine 采访 James L. 肯特，他承认“俘虏了满屋子的人...... 长达 14 小时”，称之为“狂热的迹象”。 朋友们“用网”盘旋，怀疑他在时间波上胡言乱语的真实性，他把这些胡言乱语记录在一个巨大的、涂满涂鸦的卷轴上。 他曾经说过，“我认为自己患有精神分裂症”，尽管他更喜欢从人类学（萨满教的术语）而不是精神病学的角度来构建他的状态。

​

当毒品对使用者发起攻击时

​

事实上，麦肯纳世界观中最具启发性的线索之一——后来被詹姆斯·肯特等批评家强调——是他倾向于将精神活性物质视为有意识的物质，而不是惰性化学物质。 在迷幻亚文化中，使用者经常将毒品视为朋友、老师或骗子；迷幻药、大麻或酒精会“刺激”他们。 泛灵论倾向可能会导致吸毒者向外看，看毒品本身，而不是了解自己的内在性。

阿什利·兰德 (Ashley Lande) 多年后反思自己的经历，以残酷的诚实捕捉到了这种万物有灵的动态：“LSD 是我的朋友。 我的善变、令人兴奋、可怕、虐待的朋友。” 据报道，麦肯纳后来在伦敦的一次聚会上喝了一口蘑菇茶后就失去了行动能力——显然是摄入了漂浮在表面的浓缩残留物。 他承认：“我不想害怕它。” 他将自己的处境比作一位即将退休的登山者：“攀登珠穆朗玛峰的人...... 在某些时候，他们会成为运动服装制造商或其他公司的顾问。”

与麦肯纳的严肃相反，亨特·S. 汤普森和感恩至死乐队以完全不尊重知识分子的方式对待这些药物。 尽管其代表人物的生活远非典范，但恶作剧的压力却让人们能够抵御失望和困惑。 相比之下，麦肯纳将一切都押在“英雄剂量”和宇宙启示的一致性和深刻性上，这使得当他的期望被不可预测的、有时不连贯的改变状态的现实背叛时，他特别容易受到不稳定的影响。 一种娱乐性的方式可能会让麦肯纳免受“糟糕的旅行”后的一些精神痛苦，允许灵活的应对而不是存在的崩溃。

​

公共言论与私人现实

​

恐怖之旅带来的心理打击首先到来。智力上的打击是后来发生的。

20 世纪 90 年代中期，一位名叫马修·沃特金斯 (Matthew Watkins) 的年轻数学家与麦肯纳通信，讨论了时间波零计算中的缺陷，该计算已转移到一个华丽的伪数学计算机程序，麦肯纳相信该程序最终可以矢量化他在学院中作为天才的接受度。 （虽然大部分都是无稽之谈，但麦肯纳的同事拉尔夫·亚伯拉罕表示特伦斯独立发现了混沌理论中的某些想法）。 他们于 1996 年在帕伦克研讨会上相识。 沃特金斯并不是以敌对的揭穿者的身份出现的。他真的很好奇。 然而，在几次友好的谈话中，他系统地拆解了支撑该理论的数学原理。 沃特金斯还注意到，麦肯纳图形结构中现在存在的“分形”在多年前的原始表述中并不存在，这让人怀疑内容正在改变以适应时代的狂热情绪。

时机很困难。 麦肯纳患有严重的偏头痛，几乎无法离开他的房间。 回到夏威夷后，他在自己的 Hyperborea 网站上发布了正式回应，但这次遭遇显然令他震惊。 这是第一次，有人以自己的方式挑战他的预言性数学——并获胜。

沃特金斯的批评是在恐怖之旅几年后提出的，因此它并没有导致最初的崩溃。 但这确实加深了麦肯纳对自己宏大叙事的怀疑。 他在他所谓的“数学地狱”中度过了两年时间，与物理学家约翰·谢利亚克合作挽救这一理论。 他们不能。 到那时，该理论的命运已经注定：它在数学上毫无根据。

​

表现、怀疑和注意力经济

​

尽管麦肯纳的疑虑越来越多，但他的公开讲座却变得越来越受欢迎。 然而私下里，他开始承认他在台上不愿说的话。 据报道，他对克里斯蒂安·拉奇（Christian Rätsch）和桑腾·多杰（Samten Dorje）说，他不再相信零时间波：“不。但它能支付账单。” 在某些方面，他的处境类似于亚历克斯·琼斯和拉塞尔·布兰德等当代媒体人物，他们将精心设计的想法和对当权派的怀疑货币化，无论他们私下是否相信自己的话。 正如朱尔斯·埃文斯 (Jules Evans) 指出的那样，1990 年，特伦斯每次演讲的收费相当于 7,000 美元。 就像几十年前的艾伦·瓦茨（Alan Watts）一样，他在用宇宙格言让观众眼花缭乱之前，先用伏特加麻醉自己，麦肯纳发现自己陷入了精神艺人的角色。 他知道吸引观众的风险，但无法完全避免。

特伦斯本人理解这种紧张。 当拉姆·达斯曾经告诉他，“你的生活就是你的信息”时，特伦斯有一句著名的回答：“我的生活一团糟。 我的信息就是我的信息。”

麦肯纳所谓的“多言盾牌”的特殊力量也无济于事。 特伦斯的口才非常简单，令人眼花缭乱。 他对历史、语言、毒品和意识的即兴创作长篇大论经常持续几个小时，将疯狂的想法变成华丽的、看似连贯的叙述。 在别人嘴里听起来可能有些疯狂的东西——自我改造的机器精灵、蘑菇标志和世界末日的时间分形——通过麦肯纳的雄辩，变成了引人入胜的哲学娱乐。 自《拉乔雷拉》以来，这种清晰的狂热一直保护着他，在《拉乔雷拉》中，可以说是两兄弟之间共同的精神病发作成为了一个复杂的形而上学体系的基础。 在后来的生活中，同样的言语才华使他免受审查：很少有人能够（或想要）刺破这个咒语。 麦肯纳精湛的语言技巧使他能够将原本可能被视为药物引起的疯狂的现象呈现为高深的理论。

​

进入记忆洞

​

尽管它在麦肯纳的人生故事中具有重要意义，但直到 10 月 7 日圣约翰传记发布之前，人们对其细节知之甚少。 丹尼斯最初在他的回忆录第一版中包含了一些简短的细节。 后来的版本完全删除了这段话，取而代之的是对特伦斯日益增长的矛盾心理和不经常使用迷幻药的更温和的描述。

为什么要悄悄删除？

也许有一些实际的解释。 由于丹尼斯本人仍然活跃在迷幻演讲中，他强调“英雄剂量”的预言家已经被一个可能向禁酒主义者提供弹药或让麦肯纳的追随者幻灭的人粉碎了。 但更深层次的原因可能在于迷幻布道的文化动力。 正如记者朱尔斯·埃文斯 (Jules Evans) 所指出的，“迷幻剂代言人很容易吸引观众——他们的观众往往是年轻人，他们想听听迷幻剂是如何治愈、有意义和有趣的。 他们不想听到糟糕的旅行。” 有些粉丝在第一次听到这个故事时确实被背叛了。 “对于某些人来说，卢克·天行者突然将尤达大师视为恶毒的小魔怪”，圣约翰写道。

​

是什么导致了一次糟糕的旅行？

​

另一位评论员在迷幻沙龙讨论板上写道：“我非常确定他在 1990 年之前就抨击了它，并赢得了在这种背景下谈论它的权利。” “对蘑菇失去兴趣并没有什么令人震惊的。 他刚刚击败了那件事。 最终，从特伦斯的角度来看，没有什么可以探索的了。” 尽管没有明确的证据表明麦肯纳曾直接谈论过他的“恐怖之旅”或综合了他从婚姻咨询课程中获得的教训，但他并没有回避分享他在 20 世纪 80 年代末及以后的艰难经历。 残酷的诚实是他的标志性特征之一，他经常在观众面前透露自己心理的局限性。

他越来越不愿意直接充当蘑菇的倡导者，并指出蘑菇“可能对你没有好处”，并且服用蘑菇“没有什么英雄主义”。 然而他继续重视恐怖体验本身。 “如果它不吓到你，那就不值得做，”他告诉观众。 “我不会从飞机上跳下来，我不会拍摄急流，我不会攀岩，我是一个书生气十足的人。 但我会屈服于这种恐惧，因为它似乎是有道理的。” 对于麦肯纳来说，恐怖有其独特的逻辑。 他对迷幻药保持着英雄般的态度，坚持认为“没有人滥用这些药物，除非它们做得太少。”

这种不和谐是埃文斯所说的“迷幻宗教”的症状。 它的中心教条是迷幻体验本质上是有意义的、变革性的和积极的。 那些公开质疑这一点的人——无论是通过批评还是通过不方便的个人经历——都有被忽视、边缘化或被删除的风险。 麦肯纳的案例很有启发性，因为他不是被外部权威压制，而是被他帮助发起的运动的逻辑压制。 麦肯纳周围的文化经济放大了这种动力。 他通过讲座、书籍和咨询获得了可观的收入——所有这些都与他作为“古老复兴”的机智先知的角色息息相关。

特伦斯·麦肯纳的悲剧和辉煌之处在于，这场斗争是在巨大的文化期望的重压下在公共场合发生的。 大多数经历过一次令人震惊的糟糕旅行的人都是私下里经历的，通过治疗或精神反思来整合后果。 麦肯纳必须一夜又一夜地在舞台上表演，同时保持一个已经解决了一切的人的形象。

他的多嘴盾牌可以保护他免受外部挑战，但却无法保护他免受夏威夷之行引发的内心崩溃。

因此，奇怪的是，丹尼斯·麦肯纳说过“没有糟糕的旅行”。 最近的实证研究表明我们不可能否认真正“糟糕”的迷幻体验的存在。 在一项针对 613 名使用过经典致幻剂的人的调查中，8.9% 的人报告功能障碍持续超过一天，2.6% 的人在一次充满挑战的旅行后寻求医疗或心理援助。这些数字可能看起来很小——事实上，大多数人报告没有困难的经历——但应用于数百万用户，它们表明存在不可忽视的风险。 然而，即使是最糟糕的经历，一开始看起来只是“糟糕”，但随着时间的推移，通过谈判和洞察力，也可能变得“具有挑战性”。 问问特伦斯就知道了。
